narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing you'll likely struggle with the bar exam feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for it. Our model looked at your profile and saw a 42% chance of not passing the bar, enough to pause and say this might not be the right path right now. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 4.0, right in the middle of the pack at your school, which painted a picture of grades that didn't climb as high as they needed to for someone aiming at the bar's steep challenges. Your LSAT score of 31 followed close behind, signaling to the model that the raw analytical firepower for those intense exam pressures might fall short. Even your Year 1 decile of 4.0 reinforced that early pattern, suggesting consistency but not the standout performance that often tips the scales toward success. Coming from a lower-middle family income added another layer, as the model associates that with fewer resources to buffer the grueling prep ahead. Your Asian background played a role too, tying into patterns where the bar's demands hit extra hard despite high expectations. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 didn't help, whispering doubts about foundational academic stamina. Planning to go full-time is a plus in some ways, but it still nudged the outlook toward struggle amid the workload. The one bright spot was your gender—being female actually pulled slightly toward passing, like a quiet vote of confidence in your resilience. Together, these pieces formed a story of solid effort but hurdles that, for the model, add up to real risk on the bar exam. This isn't about your worth—it's a data-driven heads-up to build those strengths first, maybe through targeted prep or experience elsewhere. You've got time to rewrite this story; many do. Keep pushing.
